core_concept.principle: Covalent Bonding: Sigma and Pi Orbital Overlap
core_concept.explanation: Covalent bonds form through orbital overlap between atoms. Sigma (σ) bonds result from head-on overlap along the bond axis and are the strongest type of covalent bond. Pi (π) bonds form from sideways overlap of parallel p orbitals above and below the bond axis. Single bonds contain one σ bond, double bonds contain one σ + one π, and triple bonds contain one σ + two π bonds.

image.alt: Diagram comparing sigma and pi bond orbital overlap
image.caption: Sigma bonds form from head-on overlap; pi bonds from sideways overlap

sections.introduction: The distinction between sigma and pi bonds is fundamental to understanding molecular structure, bond strength, and chemical reactivity. While sigma bonds provide the structural framework of molecules, pi bonds add additional bonding capacity and create regions of high electron density that drive many organic reactions.

introduction_image.alt: Visual comparison of single, double, and triple bonds
introduction_image.caption: Bond multiplicity increases from single to triple bonds

key_concept.definition: Sigma bonds are formed by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis, while pi bonds are formed by sideways overlap of parallel p orbitals perpendicular to the bond axis.
key_concept.context: All covalent bonds contain at least one sigma bond; pi bonds only exist in addition to sigma bonds in multiple bond systems.

image.alt: Detailed orbital overlap showing sigma and pi formation
image.caption: Sigma overlap is direct; pi overlap is lateral

classifications.value: Determined by geometry of orbital interaction
classifications.behavior: Sigma bonds allow rotation; pi bonds restrict rotation and lock geometry

examples.explanation: Formed by sp³-sp³ head-on overlap in ethane. Electron density concentrated along bond axis. Free rotation possible.

image.alt: Sigma bond formation between two sp³ carbons
image.caption: Sigma bond electron density is cylindrically symmetric

examples.explanation: Formed by p-p sideways overlap in ethene. Electron density above and below bond plane. Rotation restricted.
examples.value: Pi bond energy ~270 kJ/mol vs sigma ~350 kJ/mol

principles.statement: The first bond formed between any two atoms is always a sigma bond.
principles.application: Single bonds = 1σ; Double bonds = 1σ + 1π; Triple bonds = 1σ + 2π. Pi bonds cannot exist independently.
principles.example: N₂ molecule: N≡N contains one sigma bond (sp-sp overlap) and two pi bonds (p-p overlap).
principles.analogy: Like building a bridge: the sigma bond is the main support cable, while pi bonds are additional reinforcement cables.

image.alt: Diagram showing sigma bond forms first in all covalent bonds
image.caption: Sigma bond is the foundation of all covalent bonding

principles.statement: Sigma bonds are stronger than pi bonds due to greater orbital overlap.
principles.application: C-C sigma bond (~347 kJ/mol) is stronger than C=C pi bond (~270 kJ/mol), explaining why pi bonds break first in reactions.
principles.example: In alkene addition reactions, the pi bond breaks while the sigma framework remains intact.

image.alt: Energy comparison of sigma and pi bonds
image.caption: Greater overlap in sigma bonds yields higher bond energy

principles.statement: Sigma bonds allow free rotation; pi bonds lock molecular geometry.
principles.application: Single bonds permit conformational changes; double bonds enable geometric (cis/trans) isomerism.
principles.example: Ethane rotates freely around C-C; 2-butene exists as distinct cis and trans isomers.
principles.analogy: Sigma bonds are like a turnstile (rotation allowed); pi bonds are like a welded joint (rotation blocked).

image.alt: Comparison of rotation around single vs double bonds
image.caption: Pi bonds prevent rotation, enabling stereoisomerism

principles.statement: Sigma bond electron density is concentrated along the bond axis; pi bond density is above and below.
principles.application: Pi electrons are more exposed and accessible to electrophiles, making pi bonds more reactive.
principles.example: Alkenes undergo electrophilic addition because pi electrons are exposed above/below the molecular plane.

image.alt: Electron density maps for sigma and pi bonds
image.caption: Pi electron density is more exposed and reactive

components.definition: Covalent bonds are classified by the symmetry of orbital overlap relative to the internuclear axis.
components.equation: $\text{Bond Order} = \frac{\text{Number of bonding electrons} - \text{Number of antibonding electrons}}{2}$
components.interpretation: Bond order correlates with bond strength and length: higher bond order = stronger, shorter bonds. Single=1, Double=2, Triple=3.

image.alt: Relationship between bond order, length, and strength
image.caption: Bond order determines molecular properties

categories.description: The number of sigma and pi bonds in a molecule directly affects bond length, strength, and reactivity.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Adding pi bonds shortens bond length (more electron density between nuclei) but pi bonds themselves are weaker and more reactive than sigma bonds.

examples.explanation: Single bond: 1σ, 0π. Bond length 154 pm, bond energy 347 kJ/mol. Free rotation.

image.caption: Single bonds are longest and allow rotation

examples.explanation: Double bond: 1σ, 1π. Bond length 134 pm, bond energy 614 kJ/mol. Restricted rotation.

image.caption: Double bonds are shorter and lock geometry

examples.explanation: Triple bond: 1σ, 2π. Bond length 120 pm, bond energy 839 kJ/mol. Linear geometry.

image.caption: Triple bonds are shortest with cylindrical pi electron density

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison of single, double, and triple bonds
image.caption: Bond properties vary systematically with bond order

worked_examples.title: Counting Sigma and Pi Bonds in Propyne (C₃H₄)
worked_examples.problem: Determine the number of sigma and pi bonds in propyne (CH₃-C≡CH), and identify which bonds allow rotation and which restrict molecular geometry.

step_by_step.explanation: Propyne has a methyl group attached to a carbon with a triple bond to another carbon: CH₃-C≡CH
step_by_step.calculation: C₁: 4 single bonds; C₂: 1 single + 1 triple; C₃: 1 triple + 1 single

step_by_step.explanation: Every bond contains exactly one sigma bond. Count all connections between atoms.
step_by_step.calculation: C-H bonds: 4; C-C bonds: 2; Total σ = 6 sigma bonds

step_by_step.explanation: Only multiple bonds contain pi bonds. Triple bond = 2π, Double bond = 1π, Single bond = 0π.
step_by_step.calculation: C≡C triple bond: 2π bonds; Total π = 2 pi bonds

step_by_step.explanation: Single bonds (σ only) allow rotation. The C≡C triple bond locks geometry linearly.

worked_examples.final_answer: Propyne contains 6 sigma bonds and 2 pi bonds. The CH₃-C single bond allows rotation; the C≡C triple bond restricts geometry to linear arrangement.
worked_examples.concept_applied: All bonds contain one sigma bond; pi bonds only exist in multiple bonds and restrict rotation.

image.alt: Propyne structure with sigma and pi bonds labeled
image.caption: Systematic counting of sigma and pi bonds in propyne

worked_examples.title: Predicting Reactivity: Sigma vs Pi Bond Cleavage
worked_examples.problem: Explain why electrophilic addition to alkenes breaks the pi bond but leaves the sigma bond intact, and predict the product of HBr addition to ethene.

step_by_step.explanation: C-C sigma bond (~347 kJ/mol) is stronger than C=C pi bond (~270 kJ/mol).
step_by_step.calculation: Energy required: π bond < σ bond, so π breaks first

step_by_step.explanation: Pi electrons are exposed above/below the plane, accessible to electrophiles like H⁺.
step_by_step.calculation: Pi electrons act as Lewis base (nucleophile)

step_by_step.explanation: Pi bond breaks; two new sigma bonds form (C-H and C-Br). Net: 1π lost, 2σ gained.

worked_examples.final_answer: The pi bond breaks because it is weaker and more exposed. HBr adds across the double bond to form bromoethane, converting sp² carbons to sp³.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Pi bonds are more reactive than sigma bonds due to lower bond energy and exposed electron density.

image.alt: Mechanism of electrophilic addition to alkene
image.caption: Pi bond breaks while sigma framework remains intact

sections.role: Foundation of Molecular Structure and Reactivity

components.composition: Head-on overlap of hybrid orbitals (sp³, sp², sp) or s orbitals
components.function: Provides primary bonding framework; determines molecular connectivity
components.key_feature: Cylindrical symmetry around bond axis; allows free rotation

image.caption: Sigma bonds have electron density along the bond axis

components.composition: Sideways overlap of unhybridized parallel p orbitals
components.function: Adds additional bonding in multiple bonds; creates reactive electron-rich regions
components.key_feature: Electron density above and below bond plane; restricts rotation

image.caption: Pi bonds have electron density perpendicular to bond axis

function.operation: Sigma bonds form the stable molecular skeleton; pi bonds add bonding capacity and reactivity.
function.energy: Sigma bonds require more energy to break; pi bonds break first in chemical reactions, enabling addition and substitution.
function.mechanism: Orbital symmetry determines overlap efficiency: head-on (σ) maximizes overlap; sideways (π) is less efficient but enables multiple bonding.

image.alt: Functional comparison of sigma and pi bonds
image.caption: Sigma provides stability; pi provides reactivity

cycle.operation: Orbitals overlap to create bonding molecular orbitals with lower energy than atomic orbitals
cycle.purpose: Stabilizes molecule by concentrating electron density between nuclei

image.alt: Molecular orbital formation from atomic orbitals
image.caption: Bonding orbitals are lower in energy than separated atoms

cycle.operation: Energy input breaks weaker pi bonds first while sigma framework remains
cycle.purpose: Enables chemical transformations without complete molecular fragmentation

image.alt: Selective pi bond cleavage in reaction
image.caption: Pi bonds break selectively in organic reactions

cycle.operation: New sigma bonds form as pi electrons participate in reactions
cycle.purpose: Creates new molecular structures with different properties

image.alt: New bond formation after pi bond cleavage
image.caption: Reaction converts pi bonds to new sigma bonds

process_flow.description: Examine the Lewis structure to identify single, double, and triple bonds.
process_flow.result: Complete inventory of sigma and pi bonds

image.alt: Lewis structure with bond types marked
image.caption: Start by identifying all bond types in the molecule

process_flow.description: Count every connection between atoms (each bond has exactly one sigma).
process_flow.mechanism: Total σ = (number of bonds) regardless of multiplicity
process_flow.result: Sigma bond count for the molecule
process_flow.organization: Include all C-H, C-C, C-O, C-N, etc. bonds

image.caption: Every bond contributes exactly one sigma bond

process_flow.description: Add pi bonds from multiple bonds only (double=1π, triple=2π).
process_flow.mechanism: Total π = (number of double bonds) + 2×(number of triple bonds)
process_flow.result: Pi bond count indicating unsaturation and reactivity

image.caption: Pi bonds only exist in multiple bond systems

process_flow.description: Use sigma/pi count to predict rotation, strength, and reactivity.
process_flow.mechanism: More pi bonds = shorter, stronger overall bond but more reactive
process_flow.result: Understanding of molecular behavior and reaction sites

image.caption: Bond composition predicts molecular properties

primary_example.explanation: Two sp³ hybrid orbitals overlap head-on to form C-C sigma bond, releasing energy and stabilizing the molecule.

image.caption: Head-on overlap creates strong sigma bond

examples.explanation: Hydrogen 1s orbital overlaps with carbon sp³ hybrid to form C-H sigma bond.
Two sp² hybrids overlap to form the sigma component of a double bond.

image.caption: Sigma bonds form from various orbital combinations

process_types.energy_profile: Higher energy, less stable than sigma
process_types.direction: Exothermic but less so than sigma
process_types.function: Adds bonding capacity and creates reactive sites

primary_example.explanation: Two parallel unhybridized p orbitals overlap sideways to form pi bond above and below the sigma framework.

image.caption: Sideways overlap creates pi bond

examples.explanation: One pair of parallel p orbitals forms one pi bond in alkenes.
Second perpendicular pair of p orbitals forms second pi bond in alkynes.

image.caption: Pi bonds require parallel unhybridized p orbitals

sections.purpose: Understanding Sigma and Pi Bond Behavior

activation.definition: Pi bonds are activated for reaction by their exposed electron density and lower bond energy compared to sigma bonds.
activation.effect: Electrophiles attack pi electrons, breaking the pi bond while preserving the sigma framework, enabling addition reactions.

image.caption: Exposed pi electrons attract electrophiles

key_properties.explanation: Sigma bonds (347 kJ/mol for C-C) are stronger than pi bonds (270 kJ/mol for C=C pi component), explaining selective pi bond cleavage.

image.caption: Sigma bonds require more energy to break

key_properties.explanation: Adding pi bonds shortens overall bond length: C-C (154 pm) > C=C (134 pm) > C≡C (120 pm) due to increased electron density between nuclei.

image.caption: More bonds = shorter bond length

key_properties.explanation: Sigma bonds allow free rotation (~12 kJ/mol barrier); pi bonds create high rotational barrier (~270 kJ/mol) enabling stereoisomerism.

image.caption: Pi bonds lock molecular geometry

key_properties.explanation: Pi electrons are exposed above/below the molecular plane, making them more accessible to electrophiles than sigma electrons.

image.caption: Pi electrons are more exposed and reactive

summary: Sigma (σ) and pi (π) bonds represent the two fundamental types of covalent bonding through orbital overlap. Sigma bonds form from head-on overlap along the internuclear axis, are stronger, allow free rotation, and exist in all covalent bonds. Pi bonds form from sideways overlap of parallel p orbitals, are weaker, restrict rotation, and only exist alongside sigma bonds in multiple bond systems. Single bonds contain 1σ, double bonds contain 1σ+1π, and triple bonds contain 1σ+2π. The presence of pi bonds shortens bond length, increases overall bond energy, but creates reactive sites due to exposed electron density. Understanding sigma and pi bonding is essential for predicting molecular geometry, bond strength, rotational freedom, stereochemistry (cis/trans isomerism), and chemical reactivity patterns, particularly the susceptibility of pi bonds to electrophilic addition reactions in alkenes and alkynes.
